child_stage_plain_english: just past two and a half
educational_objective: Maps 'Feeling faces and story books' to the 'I'm telling you my version' doorway using current 31-33m evidence.
age_stage_nuance: What is different at 31-33 months versus the neighbouring toddler bands.
what_to_look_for: Books that support children in expressing and retelling their own versions of events and feelings. Look for stories that model healthy emotional expression and problem-solving, moving beyond simple feeling identification to narrative development.
what_to_avoid: Books that are too simplistic, only focus on naming feelings without context, or are too advanced for a just-past-two-and-a-half-year-old. Avoid products with poor reviews or unclear age guidance.
methodology: Initial search conducted on Reddit, BookTrust, Scottish Book Trust, Amazon UK, and Waterstones UK for toddler books on feelings and emotions. Candidates were filtered based on age appropriateness (31-33 months), relevance to the 'telling my version' stage, and retail proof (ratings and reviews). Top Picks were selected for their strong alignment with the stage shift and high quality evidence. Skipped candidates were identified for not meeting age or quality criteria.

top_picks.product_name: Ruby’s Worry: A Big Bright Feelings Book
top_picks.product_description_under_30_words: A Big Bright Feelings picture book where Ruby’s worry grows and grows until she finds the courage to tell someone, told in warm pages for shared bedtime reading.
top_picks.ember_verdict: Just past two and a half, feelings start arriving as little stories they want to tell, not only faces to name. Ruby’s growing worry gives you a gentle plot to talk through their own version. Sharing big worries at bedtime without turning the evening into a feelings chart.
top_picks.personalization_hint: For a child who tends to keep worries to themselves, leading to big feelings.
top_picks.why_it_fits: The narrative arc of Ruby's worry growing and then shrinking when shared directly supports the 'telling my version' objective, showing the power of verbalizing feelings and experiences. This is a significant step up from simply pointing to feeling faces.
top_picks.gift_note: Thoughtful if age-fit and not already owned.
top_picks.ownership_note: High chance the family owns a version already. Look for the stage shift: a version that adds clearer turns, richer pretend play, more control or easier setup rather than simply more of the same.
top_picks.review_quality_note: Strong multi-source proof from Amazon UK and Waterstones UK.

top_picks.product_name: Ravi's Roar: A Big Bright Feelings Book
top_picks.product_description_under_30_words: A lively Big Bright Feelings picture book where Ravi turns into a tiger when he is angry, then finds his way back after the roar, in bright pages for short shared reads.
top_picks.ember_verdict: Anger can arrive as a whole body roar before words catch up. Ravi’s tiger story gives a shared picture for that rush and the softer return after. Angry roar nights get a kind story frame, so you can talk about their version without a behaviour chat on the sofa.
top_picks.personalization_hint: For a child who has big reactions when things don't go their way.
top_picks.why_it_fits: Ravi's transformation into a tiger vividly portrays intense anger, offering a concrete way for children to understand and articulate their own feelings. The resolution, where Ravi learns to make amends, directly supports the 'telling my version' stage by modeling how to process and communicate after a big emotional outburst.
top_picks.gift_note: Thoughtful if age-fit and not already owned.
top_picks.ownership_note: High chance the family owns a version already. Look for the stage shift: a version that adds clearer turns, richer pretend play, more control or easier setup rather than simply more of the same.
top_picks.review_quality_note: Strong multi-source proof from Amazon UK and Waterstones UK.

top_picks.product_name: The Great Big Book of Feelings
top_picks.product_description_under_30_words: A large picture book that walks through lots of everyday feelings in different situations, with illustrations that invite pointing, naming and short chats as you turn the pages.
top_picks.ember_verdict: Once they can name happy and sad, the next step is a wider set of words for sticky evenings. This big feelings book spreads situations across pages so they can pick the one that matches their version. Many feeling words without pretending one emotion chart covers the whole day.
top_picks.personalization_hint: For a child who is starting to ask 'why' they feel a certain way.
top_picks.why_it_fits: By presenting a wide array of feelings in different contexts, this book helps children build a richer vocabulary for their emotional experiences. This supports the 'telling my version' stage by giving them more tools to articulate their internal states and connect them to external events, moving beyond basic happy/sad to more nuanced expressions.
top_picks.caveats: Age range starts at 4 years on BookTrust, but content is suitable for advanced 31-33 month olds with adult guidance.
top_picks.gift_note: Thoughtful if age-fit and not already owned.
top_picks.ownership_note: High chance the family owns a version already. Look for the stage shift: a version that adds clearer turns, richer pretend play, more control or easier setup rather than simply more of the same.
top_picks.review_quality_note: Editorial support from BookTrust and positive reviews from Amazon US (though not UK specific).
top_picks.substitute_if_unavailable: In My Heart: A Book of Feelings

top_picks.product_name: In My Heart: A Book of Feelings
top_picks.product_description_under_30_words: An interactive feelings picture book that links emotions like happiness, sadness, bravery and anger to vivid heart images, inviting your child to talk as you read together.
top_picks.ember_verdict: Telling their version needs a bridge between the feeling inside and words out loud. Heart images and short pages keep that chat concrete on tired evenings. Heart words chat when you want them in the conversation, not only listening to an adult explain how they should feel.
top_picks.personalization_hint: For a child who enjoys pointing and naming things, and is ready to connect feelings to their body.
top_picks.why_it_fits: The book's structure, with different feelings represented by unique illustrations and descriptions, provides a scaffold for children to articulate their own emotional experiences. This directly supports the 'telling my version' stage by offering a framework for expressing complex internal states in a relatable way.
top_picks.gift_note: Thoughtful if age-fit and not already owned.
top_picks.ownership_note: High chance the family owns a version already. Look for the stage shift: a version that adds clearer turns, richer pretend play, more control or easier setup rather than simply more of the same.
top_picks.substitute_if_unavailable: The Great Big Book of Feelings

top_picks.age_mark_on_listing: No specific age, but described as for young children.
top_picks.product_description_under_30_words: A gentle picture book about a little dinosaur whose anxious thoughts try to spoil a picnic, until help and kind words bring him back to enjoying the day.
top_picks.ember_verdict: Worry often shows up as a busy head before a fun plan. The Worrysaurus gives a picnic sized story for that wobble and the soft way through it. Picnic worry talk for evenings when they need their version heard, without a heavy coping talk at bedtime.
top_picks.personalization_hint: For a child who tends to overthink or gets anxious about new situations.
top_picks.why_it_fits: This book provides a clear storyline where a character experiences and overcomes worry, offering a model for children to understand and articulate their own anxieties. The emphasis on parental support in managing feelings reinforces the 'telling my version' objective by showing how communication can help alleviate big emotions.
top_picks.gift_note: Thoughtful if age-fit and not already owned.
top_picks.ownership_note: High chance the family owns a version already. Look for the stage shift: a version that adds clearer turns, richer pretend play, more control or easier setup rather than simply more of the same.
top_picks.review_quality_note: Strong editorial support and good implied ratings from search snippets.

guidance_notes: For this age band, focus on books that not only help children identify feelings but also provide a narrative framework for them to 'tell their own version' of events and emotions. Look for stories that model how characters process and communicate their feelings, moving beyond simple naming to understanding cause and effect.

Consider borrowing books from the library first, especially if you're unsure about your child's engagement with a particular theme or author. Many of these books are widely available and can be a great way to test the waters before purchasing.

If your child already has books about basic emotions, look for options that introduce more nuanced feelings or complex social situations to support their developing narrative skills and emotional intelligence.
